title: Forgetful causal masking makes causal language models better zero-shot learners

structured.summary: The paper introduces Forgetful Causal Masking (FCM), a technique aimed at improving the zero-shot and few-shot learning capabilities of causal language models like PaLM without adding computational costs. By randomly masking certain past tokens during pre-training, the authors claim improved representation learning and performance on benchmarks such as SuperGLUE.

strengths.evidence: The proposed FCM technique significantly improves PaLM’s zero-shot performance on the SuperGLUE benchmark from 55.7 to 59.2 and fine-tuning performance from 67.0 to 68.7.
FCM is shown to work effectively across different model sizes from 128M to 8B parameters, indicating its scalability.

weaknesses.evidence: The paper mentions using a mask ratio of 0.15 without discussing why this specific value was chosen or how sensitive the results are to variations.
The paper does not offer a strong theoretical foundation for why the FCM technique works, relying mainly on empirical results.
There are no reported ablation studies to demonstrate the specific impact of FCM components on performance gains.

questions.question: How does the choice of mask ratio affect performance?
questions.motivation: Understanding the sensitivity of FCM's performance to mask ratio variations would help in optimizing the approach for different scenarios.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Detailed Methodological Justifications. The paper mentions using a mask ratio of 0.15 without discussing why this specific value was chosen or how sensitive the results are to variations
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient Theoretical Underpinning. The paper does not offer a strong theoretical foundation for why the FCM technique works, relying mainly on empirical results
critique_points.point_003: Missing Ablation Studies. There are no reported ablation studies to demonstrate the specific impact of FCM components on performance gains
